quote_last: . The whole day had collapsed into that single fact.
title: Test — Short, time in middle
author: LitClock Test Suite

quote_last: , lying in the dark with her eyes open, rehearsing conversations that would never take place and revising arguments that had already been lost.
title: Test — Medium, time early
author: LitClock Test Suite

quote_first: The clock on the mantelpiece showed
quote_last: when he finally set down his pen. He had written six pages, crossed out four of them, and arrived at more or less the same conclusion he had started with that morning. The fire had burned low. Outside, the street was quiet in that particular way streets become quiet after midnight — not silence exactly, but an absence of the usual noise, as though the city had simply forgotten for a moment to keep up appearances.
title: Test — Long, time early
author: LitClock Test Suite

quote_last: every morning for thirty-one years, hung his coat on the same hook, filled his kettle from the same tap, and sat at the same desk facing the same window through which the same plane tree could be seen in all its seasonal variations. He had watched that tree bud and leaf and flame and strip itself bare more times than he could count. He knew the particular slant of winter light across the filing cabinets, the exact pitch of the radiator's morning clank, the smell of the building on damp Tuesdays. These things did not comfort him. But they were his, and he was theirs, and that, he had long since decided, was sufficient.
title: Test — Very long (500+ chars)
author: LitClock Test Suite

HORATIO: I think it lacks of
quote_last:
.
MARCELLUS: No, it is struck.
HORATIO: Indeed? I heard it not.

the ravens left the tower,
the bells stopped mid-peal,
